stem: A 34-year-old man with schizophrenia has been psychiatrically stable on olanzapine 20 mg daily for 8 months since his first episode. He attends a routine metabolic review. Lifestyle interventions including dietitian input and an exercise program have been in place for 4 months without meaningful weight reduction. He reports no psychiatric symptoms and is functioning well at his supported employment.

data_table.Investigations: Weight 98 kg (baseline 84 kg), BMI 33.2 kg/m2, waist 108 cm, fasting glucose 6.3 mmol/L, HbA1c 42 mmol/mol, triglycerides 2.8 mmol/L, total cholesterol 6.1 mmol/L, HDL 0.9 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Add metformin 500 mg twice daily and continue olanzapine at current dose
options.rationale: In a psychiatrically stable patient with metabolic syndrome despite adequate lifestyle intervention, adjunctive metformin is first-line pharmacotherapy before considering antipsychotic switch. Switching risks psychiatric relapse within the first 12 months after a first episode. (Source: RANZCP Metabolic Monitoring Guidelines)

options.text: Cross-taper to aripiprazole 15 mg daily over 4 weeks with psychiatric review
options.rationale: Switching to a metabolically neutral antipsychotic is appropriate if pharmacological metabolic management fails, but is not first-line given the high relapse risk in first-episode psychosis within 12 months. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Reduce olanzapine to 10 mg daily and monitor metabolic parameters at 6 weeks
options.rationale: Dose reduction risks symptom relapse and is not a standard metabolic management strategy. The metabolic effects of olanzapine are not clearly dose-dependent within the therapeutic range. (Source: RANZCP Metabolic Monitoring Guidelines)

options.text: Add orlistat 120 mg three times daily with fat-soluble vitamin supplementation
options.rationale: Orlistat is second-line for antipsychotic-associated weight gain with less robust evidence than metformin. GI side effects may reduce medication adherence. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Refer for bariatric surgery assessment given his BMI exceeds 30 kg/m2
options.rationale: Bariatric surgery referral is premature before trialling pharmacological weight management. Australian criteria typically require BMI >40 or >35 with comorbidities after failed pharmacotherapy. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guidelines for Obesity)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guidelines for Obesity

explanation.summary: Antipsychotic-associated metabolic syndrome requires a stepwise approach: lifestyle intervention first, then adjunctive pharmacotherapy with metformin, then consideration of antipsychotic switch if both fail. In first-episode psychosis within 12 months of stabilisation, the risk of psychiatric relapse from switching is substantial. Metformin has the strongest evidence base for reducing weight and improving insulin resistance in antipsychotic-treated patients.
key_points: Metformin is first-line pharmacotherapy for antipsychotic-associated metabolic syndrome after lifestyle measures fail
Antipsychotic switching is reserved for refractory metabolic effects or metformin contraindication
First-episode psychosis patients have highest relapse risk in the first 12 months
Metabolic monitoring: weight, waist, fasting glucose, lipids and BP at baseline, 6, 12 weeks then 6-monthly
explanation.pearls: Metformin 500 mg BD reduces antipsychotic-associated weight by 2-3 kg over 12 weeks and improves insulin sensitivity independent of weight loss, making it appropriate even when BMI reduction is modest.

sources.label: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guidelines for Obesity

stem: A 42-year-old woman with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been stable on clozapine 450 mg daily for 3 years. She has gained 22 kg since initiation. At her 6-monthly metabolic review, her fasting glucose has risen from 5.8 mmol/L six months ago. She takes no other medications and has no family history of diabetes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 142/90 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, weight 104 kg, BMI 37.8 kg/m2
data_table.Investigations: Fasting glucose 7.2 mmol/L, HbA1c 48 mmol/mol, triglycerides 3.4 mmol/L, HDL 0.8 mmol/L, LDL 3.9 mmol/L, eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m2

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management approach?

options.text: Cease clozapine and switch to olanzapine with ongoing metabolic monitoring
options.rationale: Switching from clozapine to olanzapine would not improve metabolic risk as olanzapine has similarly high metabolic liability. Ceasing clozapine in treatment-resistant schizophrenia risks severe psychiatric relapse. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Continue clozapine, initiate metformin with lifestyle intensification and monitoring
options.rationale: With fasting glucose 7.2 mmol/L meeting diabetes threshold, initiating glucose-lowering therapy while maintaining clozapine is the priority. In treatment-resistant schizophrenia, clozapine's psychiatric benefits outweigh metabolic risks when diabetes is actively managed. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; RACGP Diabetes Management)

options.text: Reduce clozapine to 300 mg daily and repeat fasting glucose in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Dose reduction below the therapeutic level risks psychiatric relapse. Clozapine's diabetogenic effect is not strongly dose-dependent and reducing from an effective dose is inappropriate in a stable patient. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Continue clozapine and refer to endocrinology before initiating any treatment
options.rationale: Referral to endocrinology is reasonable but should not delay initiating metformin when diabetes diagnosis is clear and the patient has no contraindications. (Source: RACGP Diabetes Management)

options.text: Continue clozapine and commence a GLP-1 receptor agonist as first-line therapy
options.rationale: GLP-1 receptor agonists have emerging evidence for clozapine-associated weight gain but metformin remains first-line for new-onset type 2 diabetes per Australian guidelines. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Clozapine carries the highest metabolic risk of all antipsychotics but remains irreplaceable for treatment-resistant schizophrenia. When diabetes develops, the management priority is active glucose control with metformin while maintaining clozapine. The decision framework balances severity of treatment-resistant psychosis against metabolic morbidity, recognising both require simultaneous active management.
key_points: Fasting glucose >=7.0 mmol/L with HbA1c >=48 mmol/mol confirms diabetes
Clozapine should rarely be ceased for metabolic complications given its unique efficacy
Metformin is first-line for type 2 diabetes in antipsychotic-treated patients
3-monthly metabolic monitoring is required once diabetes diagnosed on clozapine
explanation.pearls: Clozapine-associated diabetes can present as diabetic ketoacidosis without prior hyperglycaemic symptoms in up to 25% of cases, necessitating proactive glucose monitoring every 3-4 months rather than relying on symptom reporting.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

stem: A 28-year-old woman with schizophrenia has been stable on risperidone 4 mg daily for 14 months. She presents reporting 6 months of amenorrhoea and bilateral galactorrhoea. She is sexually active with reliable contraception and a negative pregnancy test. She has no headaches or visual field changes. MRI pituitary is normal.

data_table.Investigations: Prolactin 2680 mIU/L (reference <500), TSH 2.1 mIU/L (0.5-4.0), beta-hCG negative, FSH 2.1 IU/L (low), LH 1.8 IU/L (low)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reduce risperidone to 2 mg daily and reassess prolactin levels at 6 weeks
options.rationale: Reducing to 2 mg risks subtherapeutic dosing in a patient previously requiring 4 mg for stability. Dose reduction is reasonable only if the current dose exceeds minimum effective dose. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Add cabergoline 0.25 mg twice weekly to directly lower prolactin levels
options.rationale: Dopamine agonists like cabergoline directly counteract antipsychotic dopamine blockade and can exacerbate psychosis. They are contraindicated with antipsychotic therapy. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Switch to aripiprazole with a gradual cross-taper over 4 weeks
options.rationale: Aripiprazole is a partial dopamine agonist that does not elevate prolactin and often lowers it. Switching is the preferred strategy when hyperprolactinaemia causes clinically significant symptoms in a stable patient. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Add low-dose aripiprazole 5 mg daily as adjunct to current risperidone
options.rationale: Adjunctive aripiprazole can reduce prolactin but introduces polypharmacy and is second-line to switching when the primary antipsychotic can be replaced. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Continue risperidone and initiate combined oral contraceptive for bone protection
options.rationale: Oestrogen replacement addresses bone risk but does not treat the underlying hyperprolactinaemia or galactorrhoea. Addressing the cause is preferred over managing the consequence. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

explanation.summary: Risperidone causes dose-dependent prolactin elevation due to potent D2 blockade and poor blood-brain barrier penetration at the pituitary. Sustained hyperprolactinaemia causes hypogonadism, galactorrhoea, sexual dysfunction, and long-term osteoporosis risk. The preferred management is switching to a prolactin-sparing antipsychotic, with aripiprazole being first-choice due to its partial D2 agonism.
key_points: Risperidone and paliperidone cause the highest rates of hyperprolactinaemia among SGAs
Aripiprazole is preferred switch option due to partial D2 agonism
Dopamine agonists are contraindicated with antipsychotic therapy
Sustained hyperprolactinaemia increases osteoporosis risk warranting DEXA if prolonged
explanation.pearls: Adjunctive aripiprazole 2.5-5 mg daily normalises prolactin in approximately 80% of patients within 4 weeks without compromising antipsychotic efficacy, making it a reasonable alternative when full switch is too risky.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

stem: A 22-year-old man has been started on quetiapine 400 mg daily for a first episode of psychosis 6 weeks ago. He had normal baseline metabolic parameters. He presents for his scheduled 6-week metabolic review with no symptoms and feels well.

data_table.Investigations: Weight 79 kg (baseline 76 kg), BMI 25.8 kg/m2, fasting glucose 5.1 mmol/L, total cholesterol 4.8 mmol/L, triglycerides 1.4 mmol/L, HDL 1.2 mmol/L

lead_in: When should the next comprehensive metabolic assessment be performed?

options.text: At 8 weeks from initiation given the early weight gain trajectory observed
options.rationale: While 3 kg gain in 6 weeks is concerning, bringing forward review to 8 weeks is not part of standard monitoring. The 12-week timepoint is the next standard assessment. (Source: RANZCP Metabolic Monitoring Guidelines)

options.text: At 3 months from initiation as per standard monitoring protocol schedule
options.rationale: The standard Australian protocol specifies 12 weeks from initiation as the next assessment after 6 weeks. While the difference from 3 months is minor, 12 weeks is the specific RANZCP timepoint. (Source: RANZCP Metabolic Monitoring Guidelines)

options.text: At 6 months from initiation with weight checks monthly in between
options.rationale: Skipping the 12-week assessment misses the critical early weight gain window. Monthly weight does not replace the full metabolic panel at 12 weeks. (Source: RANZCP Metabolic Monitoring Guidelines)

options.text: At 12 weeks from initiation with weight measured at each clinical contact
options.rationale: The RANZCP metabolic monitoring framework specifies full metabolic assessment at baseline, 6 weeks, 12 weeks, then 6-monthly. Weight should be recorded at every clinical contact. This 6-week review shows 3 kg gain warranting vigilance but not schedule alteration. (Source: RANZCP Metabolic Monitoring Guidelines)

options.text: At 12 months from initiation if these 6-week parameters remain within range
options.rationale: Deferring the next assessment to 12 months is inappropriate. Metabolic changes can develop rapidly in the first 3 months of antipsychotic treatment. (Source: RANZCP Metabolic Monitoring Guidelines)

explanation.summary: The RANZCP metabolic monitoring schedule follows baseline, 6 weeks, 12 weeks, then every 6 months ongoing. The first 12 weeks represent the highest-risk period for metabolic deterioration. Weight gain exceeding 7% of baseline within 6 weeks is a red flag for intervention discussion, though it does not alter the monitoring schedule. Weight should be measured at every clinical contact regardless of the formal metabolic review schedule.
key_points: RANZCP metabolic monitoring: baseline, 6 weeks, 12 weeks, then 6-monthly
Full panel includes weight, waist, fasting glucose, fasting lipids and blood pressure
Weight gain >7% of baseline in 6 weeks warrants early intervention discussion
Weight measured at every clinical contact regardless of formal review schedule
explanation.pearls: The majority of antipsychotic-associated weight gain occurs in the first 12 weeks - gaining more than 5% of baseline weight by 6 weeks has a positive predictive value exceeding 70% for clinically significant long-term weight gain.

stem: A 38-year-old man on clozapine 500 mg daily for treatment-resistant schizophrenia presents with 2 days of nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. He has no prior history of diabetes. His last fasting glucose 4 months ago was 5.6 mmol/L. He appears dehydrated with deep rapid breathing.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/62 mmHg, HR 118 bpm, RR 28 bpm (Kussmaul), temperature 36.8 degrees C, SpO2 98% RA
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.18, bicarbonate 8 mmol/L, glucose 34.2 mmol/L, ketones 5.8 mmol/L, sodium 131 mmol/L, potassium 5.8 mmol/L, creatinine 148 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the most important consideration specific to this patient's psychiatric medication?

options.text: Withhold clozapine until metabolic derangement resolves then reassess indication
options.rationale: While clozapine should be temporarily held during DKA, permanent cessation based on a single episode is not warranted in treatment-resistant schizophrenia. It can usually be resumed with diabetes management. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Continue clozapine throughout the acute illness to prevent psychotic relapse
options.rationale: Continuing clozapine during DKA is inappropriate. Dehydration alters drug clearance and clozapine may be contributing to the crisis. Temporary withholding is standard. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Switch immediately to aripiprazole as clozapine is permanently contraindicated
options.rationale: DKA does not permanently contraindicate clozapine. Switching during acute illness risks psychiatric decompensation. Medication decisions occur after stabilisation. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Halve the clozapine dose and add long-acting insulin to prevent further episodes
options.rationale: Long-acting insulin is not used in acute DKA management - IV insulin infusion is required. Dose decisions should not be made during acute crisis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Temporarily hold clozapine, treat DKA per protocol, plan rechallenge with diabetes management
options.rationale: Temporarily hold clozapine, manage DKA with standard IV insulin and fluid protocol, then plan rechallenge with concurrent diabetes management. Clozapine remains indicated even after DKA but requires intensified glucose monitoring. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

explanation.summary: Clozapine-associated DKA can present without prior diabetes diagnosis. The mechanism involves direct pancreatic beta-cell toxicity and insulin resistance. Management requires treating DKA per standard protocol while temporarily holding clozapine, then carefully reintroducing it with concurrent diabetes management. The psychiatric team should be involved in discharge planning given clozapine's irreplaceable role.
key_points: Clozapine-associated DKA can present without prior hyperglycaemia
Standard DKA protocol applies regardless of aetiology
Clozapine not permanently contraindicated after DKA in treatment-resistant schizophrenia
Rechallenge requires concurrent diabetes management and glucose monitoring every 1-2 weeks
explanation.pearls: Clozapine-associated DKA has reported mortality of 15-30% when unrecognised versus 1-5% for typical DKA - largely because prodromal symptoms (nausea, confusion, tachycardia) are attributed to clozapine side effects rather than metabolic decompensation.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

stem: A 56-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on lithium 900 mg daily for 12 years with no relapses. At annual review, her eGFR has declined from 72 mL/min/1.73m2 last year to 58 mL/min/1.73m2. Her lithium level is 0.7 mmol/L. She has no toxicity symptoms. Blood pressure is well controlled on perindopril.

data_table.Investigations: eGFR 58 mL/min/1.73m2, creatinine 96 micromol/L, lithium 0.7 mmol/L, urine PCR 22 mg/mmol (normal <30), calcium 2.48 mmol/L, TSH 5.8 mIU/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer to nephrology and continue lithium with renal monitoring every 3 months
options.rationale: An eGFR decline to <60 warrants nephrology referral to assess for lithium-associated nephropathy. Lithium should not be reflexively ceased as the nephropathy is usually slowly progressive and continuing with specialist co-management is appropriate when mood stability is excellent. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders; KHA-CARI)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

options.text: Cease lithium immediately and switch to valproate to prevent further renal decline
options.rationale: Abrupt lithium cessation carries 50% relapse risk within 3 months. The nephropathy is typically chronic and slowly progressive - eGFR 58 warrants investigation but not emergency cessation. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

options.text: Reduce lithium dose to achieve a level of 0.4 mmol/L to slow renal deterioration
options.rationale: Reducing to 0.4 mmol/L places the patient below accepted maintenance range (0.6-0.8) and risks relapse. Dose adjustment should follow nephrology consultation rather than an arbitrary subtherapeutic target. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Repeat eGFR in 2 weeks to confirm decline before taking any further action
options.rationale: Simply repeating eGFR without specialist referral is insufficient when the level has crossed below 60. This triggers CKD stage 3b and warrants nephrology involvement. (Source: KHA-CARI Guidelines)

options.text: Add amiloride to reduce lithium-associated nephrogenic diabetes insipidus
options.rationale: Amiloride treats nephrogenic DI (polyuria/polydipsia) but this patient has no such symptoms. The concern here is declining GFR from tubulointerstitial nephritis. Amiloride does not prevent CKD progression. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Lithium-associated nephropathy affects approximately 15-20% of patients on lithium for more than 15 years. When eGFR declines below 60, nephrology referral is indicated to characterise the nephropathy and advise on lithium continuation versus planned transition. Abrupt cessation carries substantial psychiatric risk and is rarely appropriate unless acute toxicity occurs.
key_points: eGFR <60 warrants nephrology referral for lithium-treated patients
Lithium nephropathy is typically chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis
Abrupt cessation carries 50% relapse risk within 3 months
Planned taper over 2-4 weeks reduces relapse risk compared to abrupt cessation
explanation.pearls: Lithium nephrotoxicity risk increases significantly after 10 years of continuous use - annual eGFR monitoring should be accompanied by early morning urine osmolality assessment as nephrogenic diabetes insipidus often precedes GFR decline.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

stem: A 44-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on lithium 750 mg daily for 5 years. At routine monitoring, her TSH is 12.4 mIU/L with free T4 of 8.2 pmol/L. Previous TSH 6 months ago was 6.8 mIU/L. She reports fatigue and cold intolerance over 3 months. No goitre on examination. Lithium level is 0.72 mmol/L and mood is euthymic.

data_table.Investigations: TSH 12.4 mIU/L (0.5-4.0), free T4 8.2 pmol/L (10-20), lithium 0.72 mmol/L, anti-TPO antibodies positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Cease lithium and switch to lamotrigine for mood stabilisation
options.rationale: Cessation of lithium for hypothyroidism is not indicated when thyroid dysfunction is readily treatable with levothyroxine. Lithium remains gold-standard and should be continued in a stable patient. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

options.text: Initiate levothyroxine 50 mcg daily and continue lithium at current dose
options.rationale: Overt hypothyroidism on lithium is managed by adding levothyroxine while continuing lithium. Starting dose 50 mcg is appropriate for this age and TSH elevation. Recheck TSH at 6-8 weeks for dose titration. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology; RANZCP)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

options.text: Reduce lithium dose to 500 mg daily and recheck TSH in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Lithium-associated hypothyroidism is not reliably dose-dependent and dose reduction risks subtherapeutic mood stabilisation. Anti-TPO positivity suggests concurrent autoimmune thyroiditis persisting regardless of dose. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Monitor TSH at 3-monthly intervals and treat only if TSH exceeds 20 mIU/L
options.rationale: A threshold of 20 for treatment is inconsistent with guidelines. This patient has overt hypothyroidism (elevated TSH with low free T4) with symptoms - treatment is indicated now. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Initiate levothyroxine 100 mcg daily for rapid correction given symptomatic state
options.rationale: Starting at 100 mcg is too aggressive for routine hypothyroidism. Rapid correction is reserved for myxoedema coma. Standard starting dose is 50 mcg with titration every 6-8 weeks. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Lithium-associated hypothyroidism affects 20-30% of long-term users, with women at higher risk. The mechanism involves inhibition of thyroid hormone synthesis and release. When overt hypothyroidism develops, levothyroxine supplementation is standard while continuing lithium. Anti-TPO positivity identifies patients with concurrent Hashimoto's who may progress regardless.
key_points: Lithium hypothyroidism occurs in 20-30% of long-term users, more common in women
Overt hypothyroidism requires levothyroxine replacement
Lithium need not be ceased for manageable hypothyroidism
Anti-TPO positivity suggests concurrent autoimmune thyroiditis
explanation.pearls: Patients with pre-existing anti-TPO antibodies have a 6-fold higher risk of developing overt hypothyroidism on lithium, warranting 3-monthly rather than 6-monthly TSH monitoring from initiation.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

stem: A 32-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder is 28 weeks pregnant on lithium 600 mg daily maintained throughout pregnancy after careful risk-benefit discussion. Her mood has remained stable with monthly lithium monitoring. She asks about management approaching delivery.

data_table.Investigations: Lithium 0.68 mmol/L (12h post-dose), eGFR >90, TSH 2.8 mIU/L, fetal growth scan normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan for lithium management around delivery?

options.text: Cease lithium at 36 weeks to avoid neonatal toxicity and restart postpartum
options.rationale: Cessation at 36 weeks removes mood stabilisation during the highest-risk period for relapse (peripartum) and does not eliminate neonatal exposure if delivery occurs before clearance. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Guidelines)

options.text: Continue lithium unchanged through delivery with neonatal lithium level at birth
options.rationale: Continuing unchanged fails to account for the acute GFR drop and fluid shifts during labour which can precipitate maternal toxicity. Dose adjustment or withholding at delivery onset is required. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Guidelines)

options.text: Withhold lithium at labour onset, monitor levels postpartum, restart at pre-pregnancy dose
options.rationale: Standard recommendation is to withhold lithium at labour onset (or night before planned delivery). Postpartum, GFR drops rapidly causing levels to rise. Restarting at pre-pregnancy dose with daily levels for the first week is safest. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Guidelines; NICE CG192)

options.text: Switch to valproate at 32 weeks as it has a better safety profile in late pregnancy
options.rationale: Valproate has worse teratogenicity than lithium and is contraindicated in pregnancy under current TGA recommendations. Switching introduces unnecessary risk. (Source: TGA; RANZCOG)

options.text: Halve lithium dose at 34 weeks and maintain the reduced dose through delivery
options.rationale: Arbitrary halving at 34 weeks risks subtherapeutic levels before delivery and may still be too high postpartum when GFR decreases. Dynamic peripartum management is needed. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Lithium management around delivery requires understanding physiological clearance changes. During pregnancy, increased GFR causes levels to drop. At delivery, GFR returns rapidly to pre-pregnancy levels, causing accumulation if the pregnancy dose is maintained. Withholding at labour onset and monitoring closely postpartum is the safest approach.
key_points: Withhold lithium at labour onset or night before planned delivery
GFR drops postpartum causing lithium levels to rise
Monitor daily for the first week postpartum
Restart at pre-pregnancy dose, not the higher pregnancy dose
Fortnightly monitoring from 36 weeks due to variable late-pregnancy GFR
explanation.pearls: Renal lithium clearance increases by 30-50% during pregnancy then falls abruptly within 24 hours of delivery - maintaining the pregnancy dose postpartum can cause levels to rise by 50-100%, making daily monitoring for 5-7 days essential.

stem: A 62-year-old man with bipolar I disorder on lithium 1000 mg daily presents with 3 days of diarrhoea from viral gastroenteritis. He continued taking lithium as usual. He is drowsy but rousable with coarse tremor and unsteady gait. His usual level 6 weeks ago was 0.8 mmol/L. He also takes perindopril 10 mg and hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/64 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, temperature 37.1 degrees C
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 2.4 mmol/L, sodium 132 mmol/L, potassium 3.2 mmol/L, creatinine 186 micromol/L (baseline 92), urea 14.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most important initial management step?

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 50 g orally to reduce ongoing lithium absorption
options.rationale: Activated charcoal does not bind lithium and is ineffective. With 3 days of gastroenteritis, absorption of recent doses is likely complete. (Source: Austin Health Clinical Toxicology)

options.text: Commence haemodialysis given severe toxicity with neurotoxicity signs
options.rationale: Haemodialysis is indicated for levels >4.0 or severe neurotoxicity unresponsive to conservative management. At 2.4 with moderate symptoms, aggressive IV fluids and cessation should be tried first. (Source: EXTRIP Guidelines; Austin Health Clinical Toxicology)

options.text: Administer sodium polystyrene sulfonate to enhance lithium elimination
options.rationale: Resonium is for hyperkalaemia, not lithium toxicity. It does not bind lithium and would not enhance elimination. (Source: Austin Health Clinical Toxicology)

options.text: Cease lithium, withhold hydrochlorothiazide, and commence aggressive IV normal saline
options.rationale: This is dehydration-precipitated toxicity compounded by thiazide use (increases lithium reabsorption). Key steps: cease lithium, withhold thiazide, and restore volume with IV saline to improve renal lithium clearance. Most moderate toxicity resolves with this approach. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology; Austin Health Clinical Toxicology)

options.text: Administer IV sodium bicarbonate to alkalinise urine and enhance lithium excretion
options.rationale: Urinary alkalinisation does not enhance lithium excretion. Lithium is reabsorbed in the proximal tubule via sodium transporters, not pH-dependent mechanisms. Normal saline sodium loading is beneficial. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: Lithium toxicity commonly develops with volume depletion or medications impairing renal clearance. Thiazides increase proximal tubular lithium reabsorption making them a major risk factor. Initial management is cessation of lithium and offending medications, aggressive IV saline, and serial monitoring. Haemodialysis is reserved for levels above 4.0 or refractory severe neurotoxicity.
key_points: Thiazides increase lithium reabsorption and commonly precipitate toxicity
Volume depletion from any cause reduces renal lithium clearance
IV normal saline provides sodium to compete with lithium at proximal tubule
Haemodialysis for levels >4.0 or refractory severe neurotoxicity
Charcoal and urinary alkalinisation are both ineffective for lithium
explanation.pearls: Lithium has significant tissue redistribution - a falling serum level can rebound as lithium re-equilibrates from intracellular stores, necessitating serial levels every 6 hours for at least 24 hours after initial normalisation.

stem: A 24-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on sodium valproate 1500 mg daily for 2 years. She is in a new relationship and uses condoms inconsistently. She has no current pregnancy plans but might want children in the future. Her last pregnancy test was negative 3 months ago.

data_table.Investigations: Valproate level 85 mg/L (therapeutic 50-100), pregnancy test negative, LFTs normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management regarding her valproate use?

options.text: Continue valproate and provide written information about reliable contraception options
options.rationale: Written information alone is insufficient under TGA requirements for valproate in women of childbearing potential. Active pregnancy prevention measures are mandatory. (Source: TGA Valproate Safety Alert)

options.text: Continue valproate with pregnancy prevention program and annual acknowledgment form
options.rationale: While a prevention program is required, continuing valproate in a woman with inconsistent contraception and future pregnancy plans warrants discussion about transitioning to a safer alternative. (Source: TGA Valproate Safety Alert)

options.text: Cease valproate immediately and commence lamotrigine at 200 mg daily
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation risks mood destabilisation. Lamotrigine requires slow titration from 25 mg (risk of SJS) - immediate switch at high dose is dangerous. The transition must be gradual. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Add high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily and continue valproate with effective contraception
options.rationale: Folic acid does not adequately mitigate valproate's teratogenicity - direct epigenetic effects are not folate-dependent. Folic acid alone is insufficient harm reduction. (Source: RANZCOG; TGA Valproate Safety Alert)

options.text: Discuss planned transition to alternative mood stabiliser with gradual cross-taper
options.rationale: For a young woman with inconsistent contraception and future pregnancy plans, the appropriate response is planning transition to a safer alternative (lamotrigine, lithium, quetiapine). Transition should be gradual with reliable contraception in the interim. Aligns with TGA guidance. (Source: TGA Valproate Safety Alert; RANZCP; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

explanation.summary: Valproate carries the highest teratogenic risk of all mood stabilisers with 10% major malformation rate and up to 40% neurodevelopmental effects. The TGA mandates that valproate should not be prescribed to women of childbearing potential unless alternatives are ineffective and pregnancy prevention is assured. For women with inconsistent contraception and future pregnancy plans, planned transition is the appropriate path.
key_points: Valproate: 10% major malformations, up to 40% neurodevelopmental effects
TGA requires pregnancy prevention program if continued
Lamotrigine requires 6-8 week slow titration from 25 mg daily
Gradual cross-taper over 4-8 weeks reduces relapse risk
High-dose folic acid does not adequately mitigate valproate teratogenicity
explanation.pearls: Valproate neurodevelopmental teratogenicity is dose-dependent with doses above 1000 mg daily associated with greater than 30% risk of reduced IQ in exposed offspring - this is NOT mitigated by folic acid, distinguishing it from folate-dependent neural tube defects.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

stem: A 26-year-old woman with bipolar II disorder is being transitioned from valproate to lamotrigine due to pregnancy planning. She is at week 3 of titration at 50 mg daily and develops a widespread maculopapular rash on her trunk and limbs. She has no mucosal involvement, no blistering, and no systemic features.

data_table.Vital signs: Temperature 36.9 degrees C, HR 72 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Widespread maculopapular rash on trunk and upper limbs, no mucosal involvement, no blistering, no facial oedema, no target lesions

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reduce lamotrigine to 25 mg daily and reintroduce more slowly at 12.5 mg steps
options.rationale: Any rash during lamotrigine titration mandates cessation, not dose reduction. Continuing when a rash has appeared does not prevent SJS progression. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; AMH)

options.text: Cease lamotrigine immediately and consider alternative agents for mood stabilisation
options.rationale: Any rash during lamotrigine titration requires immediate cessation as it is impossible to reliably distinguish benign drug rash from early SJS. Even without mucosal involvement, the rash mandates discontinuation. Alternatives include lithium or quetiapine. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; AMH)

options.text: Continue lamotrigine at 50 mg daily with close monitoring as benign rash is common
options.rationale: While benign rash occurs in 5-10%, it cannot be reliably distinguished from SJS prodrome. Standard of care is cessation for any rash during titration. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Add prednisolone 25 mg daily and continue lamotrigine with dermatology review
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not prevent SJS/TEN progression and may mask early warning signs. The drug must be stopped. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology)

options.text: Pause lamotrigine for 1 week then rechallenge at the same 50 mg dose
options.rationale: Rechallenge after drug rash carries significant risk of more severe reaction. If ever considered, it must be specialist-led and at much lower dose. Rechallenge at same dose is contraindicated. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Lamotrigine-associated rash occurs in 5-10% of patients with SJS/TEN in approximately 0.1%. Because early SJS cannot be reliably distinguished from benign rash, any rash during titration mandates immediate cessation. Risk factors include rapid titration, concurrent valproate, and high starting dose. Valproate inhibits lamotrigine glucuronidation, doubling its levels and increasing rash risk.
key_points: Any rash during lamotrigine titration requires immediate cessation
SJS cannot be reliably distinguished from benign rash in early stages
Concurrent valproate doubles lamotrigine levels via glucuronidation inhibition
During cross-taper from valproate, start lamotrigine at 12.5 mg with halved increments
Alternative pregnancy-compatible options: lithium or quetiapine
explanation.pearls: Concurrent valproate doubles lamotrigine half-life - during cross-taper, lamotrigine titration must use half the normal increments (12.5 mg start) and SJS risk is approximately 3-fold higher than with lamotrigine monotherapy.

stem: A 45-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 90 minutes after ingesting approximately 3 grams of amitriptyline. He is drowsy but responds to voice with dry mucous membranes, dilated pupils, and urinary retention. GCS is 13.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 94/58 mmHg, HR 128 bpm, RR 18 bpm, temperature 37.8 degrees C, SpO2 97% RA
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia 128 bpm, QRS 118 ms, R in aVR >3 mm, QTc 480 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention based on the ECG findings?

options.text: Amiodarone 300 mg IV over 20 minutes for QTc prolongation management
options.rationale: Amiodarone is contraindicated in TCA toxicity as it further prolongs QT and worsens sodium channel blockade. Class IA and III antiarrhythmics are avoided. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 10 minutes for QTc prolongation management
options.rationale: While magnesium is used for torsades de pointes, the primary concern with QRS >100 ms is sodium channel blockade. Sodium bicarbonate is the priority intervention. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol IV bolus for QRS widening exceeding 100 ms
options.rationale: QRS >100 ms indicates significant sodium channel blockade predicting seizures and arrhythmias. IV sodium bicarbonate overcomes blockade via sodium loading and alkalinisation. R in aVR >3 mm further confirms significant toxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology; Austin Health Clinical Toxicology)

options.text: Phenytoin 1 g IV loading dose to prevent seizures and narrow the QRS complex
options.rationale: Phenytoin is contraindicated in TCA toxicity. As a class IB sodium channel blocker, it potentiates cardiac sodium channel blockade. Benzodiazepines are appropriate seizure prophylaxis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus as rescue therapy for cardiovascular collapse
options.rationale: IV lipid emulsion is reserved for refractory cardiovascular collapse when standard measures fail. This patient has hypotension but is not in cardiac arrest. Sodium bicarbonate should be given first. (Source: Austin Health Clinical Toxicology)

explanation.summary: TCA overdose causes toxicity through sodium channel blockade (QRS widening, arrhythmias), anticholinergic effects, alpha-1 blockade (hypotension), and GABA antagonism (seizures). The ECG is the critical prognostic tool - QRS >100 ms predicts seizures, >160 ms predicts ventricular arrhythmias. Sodium bicarbonate is specific for QRS widening. All class I and III antiarrhythmics are contraindicated.
key_points: QRS >100 ms mandates sodium bicarbonate regardless of pH
R wave >3 mm in aVR is sensitive for significant TCA sodium channel blockade
Target arterial pH 7.45-7.55 with bicarbonate after initial bolus
Phenytoin and amiodarone are both contraindicated
Seizures treated with benzodiazepines, not phenytoin
explanation.pearls: The terminal 40 ms rightward axis shift (R in aVR >3 mm) has greater sensitivity than QRS width alone for predicting TCA-related seizures and arrhythmias - it should be specifically assessed even when QRS appears borderline.

stem: A 32-year-old woman presents 2 hours after ingesting approximately 2 grams of dothiepin. She was initially alert but develops a generalised tonic-clonic seizure in the emergency department ongoing for 2 minutes. IV access is established. Pre-seizure ECG showed QRS 112 ms.

data_table.Vital signs: Pre-seizure: BP 106/62 mmHg, HR 136 bpm, SpO2 94% during seizure
data_table.Investigations: Pre-seizure ECG: QRS 112 ms, sinus tachycardia, R in aVR 4 mm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management of the seizure?

options.text: Phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV loading as first-line anticonvulsant for drug-induced seizures
options.rationale: Phenytoin is contraindicated in TCA seizures. As a sodium channel blocker, it potentiates cardiac toxicity and can precipitate arrhythmias. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Levetiracetam 2 g IV over 15 minutes as it avoids cardiac conduction effects
options.rationale: While cardiac-neutral, benzodiazepines remain first-line for toxicological seizures due to rapid onset and proven effectiveness. Levetiracetam has limited evidence here. (Source: Austin Health Clinical Toxicology)

options.text: Diazepam 10 mg IV with concurrent sodium bicarbonate bolus administration
options.rationale: While both interventions are individually correct, the diazepam dose may be insufficient and this answer does not specify adequate bicarbonate dosing. The combination is essentially right but imprecise. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Midazolam 10 mg IV followed by sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg after seizure terminates
options.rationale: First-line is IV benzodiazepine (midazolam or diazepam). Midazolam 10 mg IV has rapid onset for drug-induced seizures. Once terminated, sodium bicarbonate addresses underlying QRS widening as seizure-induced acidosis worsens cardiac toxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology; Austin Health Clinical Toxicology)

options.text: Thiopentone 3 mg/kg IV for immediate seizure termination and airway protection
options.rationale: Barbiturate anaesthesia is last-resort for refractory status. It carries significant hypotension risk compounding TCA alpha-blockade. Requires anaesthetic support and should only follow failed benzodiazepines. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: Seizures in TCA toxicity are dangerous because resulting lactic acidosis increases the proportion of unionised TCA crossing the blood-brain barrier and binding cardiac sodium channels. This creates a vicious cycle. Management requires rapid termination with benzodiazepines followed by sodium bicarbonate to reverse acidosis and sodium channel blockade.
key_points: Benzodiazepines first-line for TCA-induced seizures
Phenytoin contraindicated - worsens cardiac sodium channel blockade
Post-seizure acidosis worsens cardiac toxicity creating positive feedback
Sodium bicarbonate follows seizure termination to address both acidosis and blockade
Propofol or barbiturates with intubation are second-line if benzodiazepines fail
explanation.pearls: Each 0.1 unit drop in arterial pH during TCA toxicity increases the free (unbound) drug fraction by approximately 10-15%, explaining why seizure-induced acidosis rapidly escalates cardiac toxicity and mandates immediate bicarbonate after seizure control.

stem: A 19-year-old woman presents 45 minutes after ingesting 20 tablets of amitriptyline 25 mg (500 mg total) in an impulsive act during an argument. She is fully alert with normal examination except mild tachycardia. She denies coingestants.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 16 bpm, temperature 36.8 degrees C, GCS 15
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia 102 bpm, QRS 86 ms, QTc 420 ms, normal axis, no R in aVR

lead_in: What is the minimum observation period before medical clearance for psychiatric assessment?

options.text: Immediate clearance as the dose is sub-toxic and the ECG is normal now
options.rationale: No TCA ingestion should be cleared immediately. Anticholinergic effects can delay absorption and patients may deteriorate. A minimum observation period is mandatory. (Source: Austin Health Clinical Toxicology)

options.text: 4 hours post-ingestion observation with repeat ECG if still asymptomatic then
options.rationale: Four hours is insufficient for TCA observation. TCAs have variable absorption delayed by anticholinergic gut slowing. Minimum recommended is 6 hours. (Source: Austin Health Clinical Toxicology)

options.text: 12 hours post-ingestion with continuous cardiac monitoring regardless of symptoms
options.rationale: Twelve hours with continuous monitoring is overly conservative for this low-dose, asymptomatic presentation with normal ECG. An asymptomatic patient with normal ECG at 6 hours can be cleared. (Source: Austin Health Clinical Toxicology)

options.text: 24 hours cardiac monitoring as standard for all deliberate TCA ingestions
options.rationale: Twenty-four hour monitoring is reserved for those who develop QRS widening, arrhythmias, seizures, or altered consciousness. Blanket 24h is not resource-appropriate when risk stratification identifies low-risk patients. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: 6 hours post-ingestion with serial ECGs if remaining asymptomatic with normal QRS
options.rationale: Standard minimum for TCA ingestion is 6 hours with serial ECGs (at 2, 4, 6 hours). If asymptomatic with normal QRS throughout, medical clearance is appropriate. Significant toxicity virtually always manifests within this window. (Source: Austin Health Clinical Toxicology; Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: Risk stratification in TCA overdose relies on ECG and observation rather than reported dose. The anticholinergic effects can delay absorption. The standard is 6 hours observation with serial ECGs. Patients remaining asymptomatic with normal QRS at 6 hours have extremely low risk of subsequent cardiac toxicity.
key_points: Minimum observation period is 6 hours post-ingestion
Serial ECGs at 2, 4, and 6 hours are standard protocol
Normal QRS at 6 hours has high negative predictive value for subsequent arrhythmia
Anticholinergic effects can delay absorption making initial normal presentation unreliable
Reported dose is often inaccurate and not sole risk stratification tool
explanation.pearls: A normal QRS (<100 ms) at 6 hours post-TCA ingestion has a negative predictive value exceeding 99% for subsequent ventricular arrhythmia - making the 6-hour ECG the key medical clearance criterion rather than reported dose.

stem: A 35-year-old man has a 10-year history of recurrent episodes. He has had 4 manic episodes and 2 depressive episodes each lasting 4-8 weeks. During manic episodes he develops grandiose and persecutory delusions and auditory hallucinations. Between mood episodes, he experienced persistent auditory hallucinations and referential delusions for 6 months while documented euthymic. He currently presents euthymic with no psychotic symptoms.
lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis based on the longitudinal history?

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder of bipolar type
options.rationale: The key criterion is psychotic symptoms for >=2 weeks in the ABSENCE of a mood episode. This patient had 6 months of hallucinations and delusions while euthymic, meeting criterion B. (Source: DSM-5; RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with psychotic features
options.rationale: In bipolar I with psychotic features, psychosis occurs ONLY during mood episodes. This patient had 6 months of inter-episode psychosis - this distinguishes schizoaffective from bipolar. (Source: DSM-5)

options.text: Schizophrenia with comorbid mood features
options.rationale: Schizophrenia with mood features applies when mood episodes are brief relative to psychotic illness. This patient has well-documented manic/depressive episodes lasting weeks. (Source: DSM-5)

options.text: Delusional disorder with comorbid mood episodes
options.rationale: Delusional disorder involves non-bizarre, encapsulated delusions with preserved functioning. This patient has hallucinations, multiple delusion types, and functional impairment. (Source: DSM-5)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder with mood episodes
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder lasts <1 month. This patient's inter-episode psychosis lasted 6 months and total illness >10 years. Neither fits brief psychotic disorder. (Source: DSM-5)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder occupies the diagnostic space between schizophrenia and bipolar disorder. The critical discriminator from bipolar with psychotic features is criterion B: psychotic symptoms for at least 2 weeks absent a mood episode. Without inter-episode psychosis, the diagnosis would be bipolar I. The distinction has treatment implications requiring both antipsychotic and mood stabiliser.
key_points: Schizoaffective requires psychosis >=2 weeks WITHOUT concurrent mood episode
In bipolar I with psychotic features, psychosis occurs only during mood episodes
Longitudinal course determines diagnosis, not cross-sectional presentation
Treatment combines antipsychotic with mood stabiliser
Bipolar type specified when manic episodes are part of presentation
explanation.pearls: DSM-5 schizoaffective criterion B requires delusions or hallucinations for at least 2 weeks absent a mood episode during the LIFETIME of illness - a single documented period of inter-episode psychosis permanently distinguishes it from mood disorder with psychotic features.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

stem: A 40-year-old woman with schizoaffective disorder, depressive type, has been stable on risperidone 4 mg daily for the past year. Psychotic symptoms are well controlled but she has developed a persistent depressive episode over 8 weeks with anhedonia, poor sleep, and passive suicidal ideation. She previously tried sertraline which triggered psychotic exacerbation.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacological augmentation strategy?

options.text: Add fluoxetine 20 mg daily with close monitoring for psychotic exacerbation
options.rationale: This patient previously experienced psychotic exacerbation on sertraline. Trialling another SSRI carries similar class risk. An alternative approach is more appropriate. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

options.text: Add lamotrigine titrated slowly to 200 mg daily for depressive stabilisation
options.rationale: Lamotrigine is effective for the depressive pole without risk of antidepressant-induced psychotic exacerbation. It has no mania-switching risk and evidence for depressive relapse prevention. Slow titration required for SJS risk. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

options.text: Switch risperidone to quetiapine 600 mg daily for combined antipsychotic-antidepressant effect
options.rationale: Switching from a well-tolerated antipsychotic controlling psychosis risks destabilisation. Augmentation is preferred over switching when the current agent is effective for its primary indication. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Add lithium 450 mg daily as augmentation for treatment-resistant depression
options.rationale: Lithium augmentation is effective for unipolar TRD but less well-evidenced as first-line in schizoaffective depressive type. Lamotrigine has better evidence for the depressive pole here. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

options.text: Increase risperidone to 6 mg daily to achieve better overall symptom control
options.rationale: Increasing antipsychotic dose is not appropriate for depressive episodes. Dose escalation targets positive psychotic symptoms. The depression requires mood-directed augmentation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder depressive type requires treatment of both psychotic and mood components. When depression emerges despite adequate antipsychotic coverage, mood-directed augmentation is needed. Lamotrigine is useful for the depressive pole without psychotomimetic or mania-switching risk. In patients with antidepressant-induced psychosis history, non-antidepressant stabilisers are preferred.
key_points: Lamotrigine effective for depressive pole of schizoaffective spectrum
Previous SSRI-induced psychosis is relative contraindication to further SSRI trials
Augmentation preferred over switching when antipsychotic controls psychosis
Antipsychotic dose escalation does not treat depression
Lamotrigine titration requires 6-8 weeks from 25 mg daily start
explanation.pearls: Lamotrigine reaches therapeutic efficacy for mood stabilisation at 200 mg daily after mandatory 6-week titration - patients must be counselled that mood improvement may take 8-10 weeks from initiation, longer than typical antidepressant timelines.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

stem: A 27-year-old woman is admitted with a 3-week episode of elevated mood, decreased sleep, pressured speech, grandiosity, and auditory hallucinations. She had a similar admission 2 years ago that resolved with olanzapine. Between episodes, she attended regular outpatient appointments documented as euthymic with no perceptual disturbances and functioning at baseline.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate diagnosis based on the longitudinal information?

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type given hallucinations during mood episodes
options.rationale: Psychosis occurring ONLY during mood episodes does not meet schizoaffective criteria. Schizoaffective requires >=2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT a mood episode. This patient has no inter-episode psychosis. (Source: DSM-5)

options.text: Schizophreniform disorder with mood features given 3 weeks duration currently
options.rationale: Schizophreniform refers to schizophrenia-like symptoms lasting 1-6 months. This patient's primary disturbance is mood (mania) with secondary psychosis, not a schizophrenia-like picture. (Source: DSM-5)

options.text: Bipolar I disorder, current episode manic with psychotic features
options.rationale: Hallucinations occur exclusively during manic episodes and resolve completely between episodes. The inter-episode period is documented as normal. Absence of psychosis between mood episodes excludes schizoaffective disorder. (Source: DSM-5; RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

options.text: Recurrent brief psychotic disorder with concurrent hypomanic symptoms
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder lasts <1 month, but this is a 3-week manic episode meeting full mania criteria. The mood disturbance is the primary diagnosis with mood-congruent psychosis. (Source: DSM-5)

options.text: Delusional disorder grandiose type with comorbid cyclothymic disorder
options.rationale: Delusional disorder features encapsulated non-bizarre delusions without hallucinations or disorganisation. This patient has hallucinations and full manic episodes far beyond delusional disorder. (Source: DSM-5)

explanation.summary: The distinction between bipolar I with psychotic features and schizoaffective disorder hinges on whether psychosis occurs independently of mood episodes. When confined to mood episodes with psychosis-free inter-episode periods, bipolar I with psychotic features is correct. Careful longitudinal documentation of inter-episode mental state is essential.
key_points: Bipolar I with psychotic features: psychosis ONLY during mood episodes
Schizoaffective: psychosis persists >=2 weeks WITHOUT mood episode at some point
Longitudinal assessment essential - cross-section cannot distinguish
Mood-congruent psychotic features typical of bipolar I mania
Treatment implications: bipolar may respond to mood stabiliser alone
explanation.pearls: Up to 50% of patients initially diagnosed with bipolar I with psychotic features are later reclassified as schizoaffective when inter-episode psychosis is documented - longitudinal follow-up with specific documentation of inter-episode mental state is the only reliable method.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Mood Disorders

stem: A 52-year-old man is referred for assessment. His wife reports 2 years of conviction that their neighbour is conducting surveillance on their home. He has contacted police multiple times and installed security systems. He works as an accountant with no performance issues and maintains friendships. He sees nothing wrong and attends only because his wife insisted. Speech is logical and goal-directed with no hallucinations or thought disorder.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management approach?

options.text: Olanzapine 20 mg daily given the chronic and fixed nature of the delusional beliefs
options.rationale: High-dose antipsychotic is inappropriate initially. Evidence supports low-dose if pharmacotherapy used, but engagement is the priority given lack of insight. High dose ensures non-adherence. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Cognitive behavioural therapy focused directly on delusional beliefs with medication deferred
options.rationale: CBT requires some engagement with the possibility beliefs may be inaccurate. This patient lacks insight completely. Building therapeutic alliance first is more realistic. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Therapeutic alliance building with gradual introduction of low-dose antipsychotic
options.rationale: Reasonable approach but framing medication as targeting distress fails when the patient is not distressed by his beliefs. Initial engagement should focus on his concerns. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Engage with the patient's concerns about safety and build therapeutic alliance first
options.rationale: Delusional disorder is notoriously difficult to treat due to lack of insight. The evidence-based approach prioritises engagement through the patient's subjective experience without endorsing or challenging the delusion. Only after trust is established can treatment be introduced. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Involuntary admission for assessment given persecutory beliefs and police involvement
options.rationale: This patient does not meet involuntary criteria. He is not at imminent risk, functions well, and his behaviour is not dangerous. Involuntary powers require immediate risk not manageable less restrictively. (Source: Mental Health Act)

explanation.summary: Delusional disorder features non-bizarre encapsulated delusions with preserved functioning outside the delusional system. Treatment engagement is the primary challenge as patients lack insight. Low-dose antipsychotics show modest benefit in approximately 50% but adherence requires therapeutic alliance. The initial approach must focus on engagement through the patient's frame of reference.
key_points: Delusional disorder: preserved functioning outside the delusional system
Therapeutic alliance is the essential first step before any treatment
Low-dose antipsychotics have modest evidence but adherence is limiting
Direct challenge to delusions strengthens them and damages rapport
Involuntary treatment rarely appropriate as patients seldom dangerous
explanation.pearls: Delusional disorder has a pharmacotherapy response rate of only 33-50% even with optimal adherence, compared to 70-80% for schizophrenia - making therapeutic engagement and functional harm minimisation the realistic primary goals.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

stem: A 23-year-old woman with no psychiatric history presents with 10 days of disorganised speech, persecutory delusions, and visual hallucinations following a relationship breakdown. She has no drug use. Medical workup including MRI brain, autoimmune encephalitis antibodies, and urine drug screen are negative. She is commenced on risperidone 2 mg daily and symptoms resolve completely within 5 days.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate diagnosis and antipsychotic duration plan?

options.text: Schizophreniform disorder with plan for 6 months minimum antipsychotic treatment
options.rationale: Schizophreniform requires 1-6 months duration. At 10 days with rapid resolution, this criterion is not met. Committing to 6 months is premature. (Source: DSM-5)

options.text: First-episode psychosis with plan for 12-24 months minimum antipsychotic treatment
options.rationale: While technically a first psychotic episode, the rapid resolution and identifiable precipitant suggest brief psychotic disorder rather than schizophrenia-spectrum. Standard 12-24 month recommendation applies to presumed schizophrenia-spectrum. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Acute stress reaction with psychotic features requiring immediate antipsychotic cessation
options.rationale: Acute stress reaction does not typically include frank psychotic symptoms. Additionally, immediately ceasing the antipsychotic risks relapse. A short course with planned taper is appropriate. (Source: DSM-5)

options.text: Schizophrenia prodrome requiring indefinite antipsychotic maintenance therapy
options.rationale: Prodrome features attenuated, not full-blown, psychotic symptoms with gradual onset. This had acute onset of frank symptoms meeting threshold, resolving rapidly. Indefinite maintenance is not indicated. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder with short-term antipsychotic and gradual taper after 1-3 months
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder: 1 day to <1 month of psychotic symptoms with full return to baseline. Short-term antipsychotic (1-3 months of stability) with gradual taper is appropriate. Prognosis is generally good with ~65% never having another episode. (Source: DSM-5; RANZCP; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Brief psychotic disorder is diagnosed when psychotic symptoms last 1 day to <1 month with full return to premorbid functioning. It has good prognosis with approximately two-thirds never having another episode. Short-term antipsychotic treatment with planned gradual taper is standard, contrasting with the 12-24 month minimum for first-episode schizophrenia-spectrum.
key_points: Brief psychotic disorder: 1 day to <1 month with full return to baseline
Good prognosis - approximately 65% never have another episode
Short-term antipsychotic (1-3 months) with gradual taper
Specify 'with marked stressor' if identifiable precipitant
Distinguish from schizophreniform (1-6 months) and schizophrenia (>6 months)
explanation.pearls: Brief psychotic disorder has 50-65% no-recurrence rate, but approximately 20% will eventually be reclassified as schizophrenia on longitudinal follow-up - warranting clear relapse planning and education even when the episode resolves completely.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

stem: A 34-year-old woman presents requesting ADHD assessment. She reports lifelong difficulty with organisation, frequently losing belongings, inability to complete work tasks, and relationship difficulties from interrupting others. She was a bright student who underachieved. She has treated anxiety on escitalopram 10 mg. Her PHQ-9 is 4 and GAD-7 is 6. She brings a primary school report noting she was a daydreamer who rarely completed work.
lead_in: What is the most important diagnostic criterion to establish?

options.text: Neuropsychological testing showing executive function deficits on computerised assessment
options.rationale: Neuropsychological testing is not required and has poor sensitivity - many adults with ADHD perform normally due to the novel test environment. Clinical history is the diagnostic gold standard. (Source: NICE NG87 ADHD)

options.text: A validated screening questionnaire score exceeding the diagnostic threshold value
options.rationale: Screening questionnaires are not diagnostic. They have high sensitivity but lower specificity. Many conditions produce elevated ADHD screening scores. (Source: NICE NG87 ADHD)

options.text: Documented symptoms causing functional impairment in two or more settings before age 12
options.rationale: DSM-5 requires several symptoms present before age 12 with multi-setting impairment. This childhood onset distinguishes ADHD from acquired attentional difficulties. Collateral evidence like school reports is essential for adult diagnosis. (Source: DSM-5; NICE NG87; RANZCP)

options.text: Positive response to a stimulant medication trial confirming the underlying diagnosis
options.rationale: Stimulant response is not diagnostic. Stimulants improve concentration in neurotypical individuals too. Using treatment response diagnostically is circular reasoning. (Source: RANZCP; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Genetic testing confirming dopaminergic pathway variants associated with ADHD phenotype
options.rationale: There is no genetic test for ADHD. While highly heritable (~74%), it is polygenic with no single causative variant. Genetic testing has no role in clinical diagnosis. (Source: NICE NG87 ADHD)

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD diagnosis requires demonstrating childhood onset (before age 12) of symptoms causing functional impairment. This distinguishes ADHD from acquired attentional difficulties. In adults, collateral evidence is essential. Women are frequently underdiagnosed because predominantly inattentive presentation is less disruptive and often compensated by intelligence.
key_points: DSM-5 requires symptoms present before age 12
Collateral evidence of childhood symptoms essential for adult diagnosis
Neuropsychological testing neither required nor sufficient
Women often present with predominantly inattentive type, under-recognised
Comorbid anxiety and depression common and may mask ADHD
explanation.pearls: DSM-5 raised age-of-onset from 7 (DSM-IV) to 12 years because longitudinal studies showed predominantly inattentive ADHD in girls frequently goes unrecognised until late primary school when academic demands exceed compensatory capacity.

stem: A 31-year-old man with confirmed ADHD combined type requires pharmacotherapy. He works as a long-haul truck driver needing 12-14 hours daily symptom coverage. He has no cardiovascular disease, no substance use history, and no psychiatric comorbidities. His BMI is 27 and blood pressure is 128/78 mmHg.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line medication considering his occupational needs?

options.text: Immediate-release methylphenidate 10 mg three times daily for flexible dosing
options.rationale: Three-times-daily dosing is impractical for a truck driver and creates peaks and troughs with rebound risk. A long-acting formulation is more appropriate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Extended-release methylphenidate with dose titration to adequate symptom coverage
options.rationale: Extended-release methylphenidate provides 8-12 hours which may not achieve the 12-14 hour coverage required for long-haul driving. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Atomoxetine 40 mg daily as it provides continuous around-the-clock coverage
options.rationale: While atomoxetine provides continuous coverage, it is less effective than stimulants and takes 4-6 weeks for efficacy. Stimulants are first-line without complicating factors. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; NICE NG87)

options.text: Dexamfetamine immediate-release 10 mg twice daily for strongest symptom control
options.rationale: Dexamfetamine IR lasts only 4-6 hours per dose - twice daily provides inadequate coverage for 12-14 hour shifts. Also higher abuse potential than the prodrug form. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Lisdexamfetamine 30 mg daily given its 12-14 hour duration of action
options.rationale: Lisdexamfetamine is a prodrug providing 12-14 hours of smooth coverage matching occupational requirements. Its prodrug mechanism provides smooth onset/offset and lower abuse potential. PBS-listed for adult ADHD in Australia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; AMH)

explanation.summary: Stimulant selection should consider required duration of coverage, abuse potential, and occupational demands. Lisdexamfetamine provides 12-14 hours of smooth coverage with lower abuse potential than immediate-release formulations. For safety-critical occupations requiring extended coverage, it is the optimal choice.
key_points: Lisdexamfetamine provides 12-14 hours as a prodrug
Extended-release methylphenidate provides approximately 8-12 hours
Immediate-release formulations last 4-6 hours
Prodrug mechanism reduces abuse potential
Atomoxetine is second-line due to lower efficacy and slow onset
explanation.pearls: Lisdexamfetamine's prodrug mechanism (enzymatic conversion in red blood cells) means it cannot be abused intranasally or IV as conversion rate is fixed regardless of route - making it preferred for patients in occupations with drug testing or those with substance concerns.

stem: A 29-year-old man with confirmed ADHD (childhood diagnosis, lost to follow-up) presents seeking treatment. He has been in residential rehabilitation for methamphetamine use disorder and has been abstinent 4 months. His counsellor supports ADHD treatment as untreated ADHD was identified as a relapse risk factor. He has no other psychiatric comorbidities.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacological approach?

options.text: Atomoxetine 40 mg daily titrated to effect as first-line non-stimulant treatment
options.rationale: Atomoxetine is preferred first-line for ADHD with active or recent substance use disorder. It has no abuse potential, no street value, and provides continuous coverage. If inadequate, carefully supervised stimulant with dispensing controls becomes second-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; RANZCP; NICE NG87)

options.text: Lisdexamfetamine as it has lower abuse potential than other stimulants available
options.rationale: While lisdexamfetamine has lower abuse potential, it is still amphetamine-based which is problematic for methamphetamine use disorder specifically. Non-stimulants should be tried first. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Methylphenidate extended-release once stabilised for 6 months of abstinence
options.rationale: Deferring ADHD treatment for arbitrary abstinence period is not evidence-based. Untreated ADHD is itself a relapse risk factor. Treatment should not be withheld during recovery. (Source: RANZCP; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Guanfacine extended-release as the safest option for substance use comorbidity
options.rationale: Guanfacine ER is approved for paediatric ADHD in Australia but not PBS-listed for adults. Atomoxetine has stronger evidence base in adult ADHD. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Stimulant medication is permanently contraindicated given methamphetamine use disorder
options.rationale: Stimulants are not permanently contraindicated. They are second-line after non-stimulant options and require safeguards (specialist prescribing, dispensing controls, urine drug screening). (Source: RANZCP; NICE NG87)

explanation.summary: ADHD and substance use disorders frequently co-occur with untreated ADHD being a significant relapse risk factor. The approach prioritises non-stimulant medications first, with stimulants reserved for non-stimulant failure under specialist supervision. Withholding ADHD treatment during recovery is counterproductive.
key_points: Atomoxetine first-line for ADHD with comorbid substance use disorder
Untreated ADHD is a relapse risk factor
Stimulants not permanently contraindicated but require additional safeguards
If stimulant needed: lisdexamfetamine preferred over IR due to lower abuse potential
Dispensing controls can be implemented for stimulants
explanation.pearls: Atomoxetine reaches full efficacy at 4-6 weeks unlike stimulants which work immediately - patients must be counselled about delayed onset to prevent premature discontinuation and pressure for stimulant switch.

stem: A 26-year-old man with known schizophrenia is brought to the emergency department by police. He is highly agitated, pacing, shouting, and has thrown a chair. He refuses oral medication. He has no drug allergies, no cardiac history, and his last QTc was normal. He takes olanzapine 20 mg daily (last dose this morning).

data_table.Vital signs: HR 112 bpm, BP 148/92 mmHg, RR 22 bpm, temperature 37.0 degrees C

lead_in: What is the most appropriate parenteral medication for rapid tranquillisation?

options.text: Haloperidol 10 mg IM as first-line parenteral antipsychotic for psychotic agitation
options.rationale: Haloperidol is no longer first-line in Australian guidelines. Droperidol has faster onset, more predictable sedation, and has largely replaced haloperidol for ABD in Australian EDs. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; NSW Health ABD Guidelines)

options.text: Midazolam 10 mg IM as benzodiazepine monotherapy for adequate sedation
options.rationale: Midazolam provides sedation but does not address underlying psychotic agitation. An antipsychotic is preferred for known psychosis. Midazolam is appropriate for non-psychotic causes or as adjunct. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Droperidol 10 mg IM with continuous cardiac monitoring for minimum 2 hours
options.rationale: Droperidol 10 mg IM is first-line parenteral rapid tranquillisation in Australian ED guidelines for psychotic agitation. Rapid onset (5-15 min IM), potent effect, and well-supported by Australian evidence (DORM trial). Cardiac monitoring for >=2 hours mandatory for QTc risk. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; NSW Health ABD Guidelines)

options.text: Olanzapine 10 mg IM as it is his usual antipsychotic avoiding polypharmacy concerns
options.rationale: IM olanzapine is contraindicated within 1 hour of parenteral benzodiazepine. Additionally, patient took oral olanzapine this morning - extra olanzapine increases sedation risk. Droperidol is preferred. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Ketamine 4 mg/kg IM for immediate dissociative sedation in this situation
options.rationale: IM ketamine is reserved for extreme agitation with imminent danger where standard agents failed. It is not first-line and requires airway management capability. (Source: NSW Health ABD Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Australian guidelines for acute behavioural disturbance follow a stepwise approach: verbal de-escalation, oral medication, then parenteral if refused. For psychotic agitation, IM droperidol 10 mg is first-line in most Australian EDs, supported by the DORM trial. Mandatory post-administration monitoring includes cardiac telemetry for >=2 hours for QTc risk.
key_points: Droperidol 10 mg IM is first-line parenteral for psychotic agitation in Australia
Onset 5-15 minutes IM, duration 2-4 hours
Mandatory cardiac monitoring minimum 2 hours post-dose
IM olanzapine + parenteral benzodiazepine combination is contraindicated
Haloperidol is second-line due to slower onset and higher EPS risk
explanation.pearls: The DORM trial demonstrated droperidol 10 mg IM achieved adequate sedation at 10 minutes in 82% versus 62% for midazolam - establishing droperidol as the dominant parenteral agent for ABD in Australian emergency practice.

stem: A 44-year-old man with schizoaffective disorder on clozapine 600 mg daily is brought to the ED with acute psychotic agitation. He has struck a security officer. Verbal de-escalation failed and he refuses oral medication. His last ECG 2 weeks ago shows QTc 508 ms. He has no other medications.

data_table.Investigations: Last ECG (2 weeks ago): QTc 508 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate parenteral medication for rapid tranquillisation?

options.text: Droperidol 10 mg IM as standard first-line regardless of the baseline QTc value
options.rationale: Droperidol is relatively contraindicated with baseline QTc >500 ms as it further prolongs QTc. Cumulative torsades risk becomes significant. (Source: NSW Health ABD Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Haloperidol 10 mg IM as it has less QTc prolongation effect than droperidol
options.rationale: Haloperidol also prolongs QTc. The butyrophenone class effect on cardiac repolarisation makes both inappropriate here. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; AMH)

options.text: Olanzapine 10 mg IM as an atypical antipsychotic with lower QTc risk profile
options.rationale: IM olanzapine still carries QTc risk. Additionally, on clozapine already - adding olanzapine introduces polypharmacy and additive cardiac effects. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Midazolam 10 mg IM as a QTc-neutral sedative agent for this patient
options.rationale: Midazolam is preferred when antipsychotics are contraindicated due to QTc >500 ms. Benzodiazepines do not affect cardiac repolarisation. Dose may need repeating at 15-minute intervals. Once sedated, psychotic symptoms can be addressed with ECG assessment. (Source: NSW Health ABD Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)
